disease.name: Adrenal Tumors
disease.note: Adrenal masses include incidentalomas (most common), functional adenomas (pheochromocytoma, aldosteronoma, cortisol-secreting), and adrenocortical carcinoma. In Kenya, diagnosis is increasing with wider access to CT imaging for other indications. Pheochromocytoma requires careful pre-operative alpha-blockade to prevent intra-operative hypertensive crisis. All functional tumors and large (>4-6cm) non-functional masses warrant surgical resection.
presentingComplaints: Incidentally found adrenal mass on scan
High blood pressure that is difficult to control
Episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and sweating
Weight gain with rounding of the face
Low potassium levels found on blood tests

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Crisis/large >6cm/malignant/metastatic -> SEVERE; small non-functional incidentaloma -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Was this adrenal mass found incidentally or because of symptoms?
What scan was done and why?
Do you have high blood pressure — how long and how many medications?
Do you get episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and sweating?
Have you ever been told your potassium is low?
Do you have muscle weakness or fatigue?
Have you gained weight recently — especially around your face and abdomen?
Do you have diabetes or high blood sugar?
Have you noticed easy bruising or purple stretch marks?
Do you have any bone pain or fractures?
Have you ever had a heart attack or chest pain?
Do you smoke or drink alcohol?
Is there any family history of adrenal tumors, MEN2, or VHL?
Do you have any other tumors (thyroid, parathyroid)?
Have you lost weight unintentionally?
Do you have any abdominal or back pain?
Have you noticed any changes in your mood or anxiety levels?
Are you on any blood thinners or anticoagulants?
Do you have a history of cancer elsewhere (lung, breast, kidney, colon)?
Do you have any symptoms of thyroid dysfunction (heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations)?

examination_findings.finding: Elevated blood pressure (often severe or labile)
Central obesity with moon face and buffalo hump (Cushing's)
Purple striae on abdomen and thighs
Palpable flank or abdominal mass (large tumors)
Signs of heart failure (if catecholamine cardiomyopathy)
Neurofibromas or cafe-au-lait spots (if NF1 associated)

bedside_scores.name: Adrenal Mass Risk Stratification (Imaging + Hormonal)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=6: adrenalectomy indicated; Score 4-5: repeat imaging and hormonal workup in 6-12 months; Score <4: low risk, discharge if stable

subtypes.name: Pheochromocytoma
subtypes.management: Alpha-blockade (Phenoxybenzamine 10mg BD or Doxazosin 2-8mg daily) for 10-14 days pre-op. Beta-blockade (Propranolol 40mg TDS) added after adequate alpha-blockade (never before). Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. MIBG scan for metastatic disease. Genetic testing for MEN2/VHL/NF1.

subtypes.name: Aldosteronoma (Conn's Syndrome)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Pre-op: Spironolactone 25-100mg daily or Eplerenone to control BP and K+. Confirm lateralization with adrenal vein sampling before surgery if CT shows bilateral nodules or normal adrenals.

subtypes.name: Cortisol-Secreting Adenoma (Cushing's Syndrome)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Peri-operative stress-dose steroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h). Gradual steroid taper post-op over 6-12 months. Monitor for adrenal insufficiency (contralateral gland suppressed).

subtypes.name: Adrenocortical Carcinoma (ACC)
subtypes.management: Open adrenalectomy with en bloc resection including periadrenal fat and lymphadenectomy. Complete R0 resection essential. Mitotane therapy post-op. Chemotherapy (EDP regimen: Etoposide, Doxorubicin, Cisplatin). Radiotherapy for R1 margins. Close follow-up with CT every 3-6 months.

subtypes.name: Non-Functioning Adrenal Adenoma (Incidentaloma)
subtypes.management: Resection if: size >4-6cm, rapid growth (>1cm/year), suspicious imaging features (HU >10, irregular margins, necrosis), or functional. Follow-up CT at 6-12 months. Hormonal workup (metanephrines, aldosterone/renin, cortisol/DST) annually for 4 years. Discharge if stable for 4 years.

bedside.name: Blood pressure monitoring (lying and standing)
bedside.indication: Orthostatic changes in pheo, sustained HTN in Conn's/Cushing's

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: LVH from long-standing HTN, arrhythmias in pheo

laboratory.name: Aldosterone/Renin Ratio (ARR)
laboratory.indication: Screen for Conn's — ARR >30 with aldosterone >15ng/dL

laboratory.name: 1mg Overnight Dexamethasone Suppression Test
laboratory.indication: Screen for Cushing's — cortisol >1.8mcg/dL indicates no suppression

laboratory.name: Cortisol, ACTHP levels
laboratory.indication: Differentiate adrenal (low ACTHP) vs pituitary (high ACTHP)

laboratory.name: Serum Electrolytes (Na, K, HCO3)
laboratory.indication: Hypokalemia in Conn's — K <3.5 with HTN is classic

laboratory.name: Creatinine, eGFR
laboratory.indication: Renal function assessment before contrast CT

laboratory.name: Chromogranin A
laboratory.indication: Neuroendocrine tumor marker — elevated in pheo

imaging.name: CT Adrenal Protocol (non-contrast + contrast-enhanced + delayed washout)
imaging.indication: Gold standard — HU <10 on non-contrast = benign adenoma; rapid washout >40-60% = benign

imaging.name: MRI with chemical shift (in-phase/opposed-phase)
imaging.indication: If CT indeterminate — signal drop on opposed-phase = lipid-rich adenoma

imaging.name: MIBG Scintigraphy (I-123/I-131 MIBG)
imaging.indication: Pheochromocytoma localization, extra-adrenal disease, and metastases

imaging.name: Adrenal Vein Sampling (AVS)
imaging.indication: Lateralization in Conn's when CT shows bilateral nodules or normal adrenals

imaging.name: FDG-PET/CT
imaging.indication: If ACC suspected — high FDG uptake suggests malignancy; also for metastatic pheo

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.indication: Assess catecholamine cardiomyopathy in pheo before surgery

management.protocolReference: AAES (American Association of Endocrine Surgeons) Guidelines for Adrenal Tumors / ENSAT Guidelines

resuscitation: For pheochromocytoma: strict BP control pre-op
Alpha-blockade: Phenoxybenzamine 10mg BD starting dose, increase by 10mg every 2-3 days to target BP <130/80
Beta-blockade: Add Propranolol 40mg TDS only after alpha-blockade (to prevent unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction)
High-sodium diet during alpha-blockade to prevent orthostatic hypotension and expand volume
IV fluids during pre-op period to maintain intravascular volume
For Cushing's: stress-dose steroids during surgery
For Conn's: correct hypokalemia (K+ supplements + Spironolactone)

surgical: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy — gold standard for benign tumors <10cm
Open adrenalectomy — for ACC, large tumors >10cm, suspected malignant, or need for en bloc resection
Cortical-sparing adrenalectomy — for bilateral tumors (MEN2, VHL) to avoid steroid dependence
nonOperative: Observation with serial imaging for non-functional adenomas <4cm
Medical management: Spironolactone/Eplerenone for Conn's if unfit for surgery

immediate: ICU monitoring for first 24h after pheochromocytoma surgery — BP and glucose monitoring
IV fluids until oral intake adequate
Steroid stress dosing for Cushing's/adrenalectomy (Hydrocortisone 50-100mg IV q8h, taper)
Monitor for hypotension (especially after pheo removal — loss of catecholamine support)
day1_3: Transition to oral steroid taper if Cushing's/adrenal insufficiency
day3_7: If ACC: discuss mitotane, MDT referral
day7_14: Steroid taper continues — usually 6-12 months for Cushing's
Check 24h urine metanephrines or aldosterone/renin to confirm biochemical cure

pathways.criteria: HU <10 on NCCT, <4cm, no hormonal activity, no growth on follow-up
pathways.treatment: Observation. Repeat CT in 6-12 months. Hormonal workup (metanephrines, ARR, DST) yearly for 4 years. Discharge if stable for 4 years.

pathways.criteria: hormonal excess (pheo/Conn's/Cushing's), size >4-6cm, or growth on follow-up
pathways.treatment: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Pre-op preparation: alpha-blockade for pheo (10-14 days), Spironolactone for Conn's, stress steroids for Cushing's.

pathways.criteria: >6cm, irregular margins, necrosis, rapid growth, metastatic, high HU (>20)
pathways.treatment: Open adrenalectomy with en bloc resection. Regional lymphadenectomy. Post-op mitotane. Chemotherapy (EDP regimen) for advanced. Radiotherapy for R1 margins.

operative_templates.indication: Functional adrenal tumor, non-functional adenoma >4-6cm, pheochromocytoma, Conn's, Cushing's
operative_templates.position: Lateral decubitus position with affected side up, kidney bridge elevated, table flexed
operative_templates.incision: Four-port technique: 10mm camera port at umbilicus, three working ports (epigastric, subcostal, flank)
steps: Patient in right lateral decubitus position
Pneumoperitoneum via Veress needle or open Hasson technique
Port placement as marked
Retract spleen medially by dividing splenorenal ligament
Identify left adrenal gland superior and medial to left kidney
Dissect adrenal gland using LigaSure/harmonic scalpel
Identify and clip left adrenal vein (drains into left renal vein)
Mobilize adrenal gland circumferentially
Place in specimen retrieval bag
Extract through enlarged port site or Pfannenstiel incision
Check hemostasis, release pneumoperitoneum, close port sites
keyLandmarks: Left adrenal vein — entering left renal vein, clip and divide early
Left kidney — adrenal gland sits at upper pole
Spleen and tail of pancreas — protect during medial retraction
Left renal artery — posterior to renal vein, protect

operative_templates.indication: Right-sided functional adrenal tumor or large adenoma
operative_templates.position: Left lateral decubitus position, kidney bridge elevated
operative_templates.incision: Four-port technique: camera at umbilicus, epigastric, right subcostal, right flank
steps: Patient in left lateral decubitus position
Port placement as marked
Retract liver medially using liver retractor
Expose inferior vena cava (IVC)
Identify right adrenal vein — short, direct drainage into IVC
Carefully dissect and clip right adrenal vein (risk of IVC injury)
Mobilize adrenal gland from liver, IVC, and upper pole of right kidney
Remove specimen in retrieval bag
Check IVC and adrenal bed for hemostasis
keyLandmarks: Right adrenal vein — short, direct to IVC, high risk of avulsion
IVC — medial boundary, protect during dissection

dischargeCriteria: Hemodynamically stable — BP within target without IV medications
Electrolytes and glucose normal or near-normal
Steroid replacement regimen (if applicable) established and patient educated
Patient educated on signs of adrenal insufficiency (if Cushing's or bilateral)

patient_education.explanation: You have a growth (tumor) on your adrenal gland — a small gland that sits on top of your kidney. These tumors can be non-functioning (not producing hormones) or functioning (producing excess hormones that cause problems like high blood pressure, palpitations, weight gain, or muscle weakness). Most adrenal tumors are benign, but some can be cancerous. Treatment depends on the size, function, and appearance on scans.
warningSigns: Severe headache, pounding heart, and drenching sweats (if pheo not yet treated)
Severe high blood pressure that does not respond to medication
After surgery: fever, wound problems, severe weakness or dizziness
If on steroid replacement: severe illness, vomiting, injury — may need extra steroids (sick day rules)
activity: After laparoscopic surgery: gentle walking from day 1, return to normal activity in 2-3 weeks
After open surgery: avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks
Gradual return to work: 2-4 weeks for laparoscopic, 6-8 weeks for open
diet: No restrictions after recovery from surgery
If on steroid replacement: do not skip meals, carry extra steroids at all times
If Cushing's: low-sugar, low-sodium diet for weight and BP control
medication: If on alpha-blockers: take as prescribed — they prevent dangerous BP spikes
If on steroid replacement: NEVER stop suddenly — this can be life-threatening
Wear a MedicAlert bracelet/necklace stating 'Steroid Dependent' if applicable
BP medications may need adjustment after surgery
followUp: Endocrinology follow-up is essential — especially for Cushing's and post-adrenalectomy
Repeat hormonal workup at 6 weeks and 6-12 months to confirm cure
Annual follow-up imaging for 4 years if non-functional adenoma was observed
ACC requires close surveillance: CT every 3-6 months

before_discharge: Steroid education and sick day rules (if Cushing's/bilateral)

after_discharge.action: Endocrine follow-up — hormonal workup (metanephrines/ARR/cortisol), BP assessment, medication adjustment
Clinical review, consider repeat CT if ACC or suspicious features
Repeat imaging (CT or US), hormonal workup
Annual review for 4 years if non-functional adenoma under observation; lifelong if ACC

when_to_return: Severe weakness, dizziness, or fainting (adrenal insufficiency)
Persistent vomiting and unable to keep down medications
New severe headaches, palpitations, or sweating (recurrence of pheo)
Any new lump in the surgical area

pathophysiologyDetail: Pheochromocytoma arises from chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla; these cells synthesize catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine) via the tyrosine-hydroxylase pathway — mutations in RET, VHL, NF1, SDHB/D drive tumorigenesis in hereditary cases
Catecholamine excess causes hypertension through alpha-1 receptor-mediated vasoconstriction and beta-1 receptor-mediated positive inotropy/chronotropy; paroxysmal release explains episodic symptoms

Aldosteronoma (Conn's syndrome) involves zona glomerulosa cells with acquired somatic mutations in KCNJ5 (40%), ATP1A1, ATP2B3, or CACNA1D that cause constitutive aldosterone secretion independent of renin-angiotensin control
Aldosterone excess increases sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron via ENaC, expanding plasma volume and suppressing renin; potassium excretion causes hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis

Cortisol-secreting adenomas arise from zona fasciculata; excess cortisol causes insulin resistance (steroid diabetes), protein catabolism (muscle wasting, thin skin), osteoclast activation (osteoporosis), and central fat redistribution
Cushing's syndrome from adrenal adenoma: ACTH-independent because the adenoma autonomously secretes cortisol, suppressing pituitary ACTH and causing contralateral adrenal atrophy

Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) involves TP53 mutations (50%), IGF-2 overexpression (80-90%), and CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) activation; the Weiss criteria score nuclear grade, mitotic rate, necrosis, and vascular invasion for malignant diagnosis
The '10% rule' of pheochromocytoma: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal (paraganglioma), 10% malignant, 10% familial, 10% pediatric, 10% without hypertension
SDHB mutations predict higher risk of malignant pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma and require lifelong surveillance for metastatic disease

Adrenal incidentalomas: 80% are non-functional adenomas, 10% are functional, 5% are ACC, 5% are other (myelolipoma, cyst, metastasis). Risk of ACC increases with size — 2% for <4cm, 6% for 4-6cm, 25% for >6cm

In Kenyan patients, adrenal masses are increasingly detected incidentally on CT for TB screening or abdominal pain; access to hormonal testing is limited, so size criteria play an outsized role in surgical decision-making
The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis suppression in Cushing's means post-operative adrenal insufficiency is expected and requires stress-dose steroids and slow taper over 6-12 months

landmarkTrials.trial: Pheochromocytoma: Pre-operative Alpha-Blockade vs No Blockade
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 40 studies including 1,600 patients with pheochromocytoma
landmarkTrials.finding: Pre-operative alpha-blockade reduced intraoperative hemodynamic instability (hypertensive episodes) from 80% to 35% and reduced mortality from 5% to 0.9%
landmarkTrials.impact: Established mandatory 10-14 day alpha-blockade before pheochromocytoma surgery as standard of care
landmarkTrials.reference: Manger WM, et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1999;84(8):2737-2745

landmarkTrials.trial: ENSAT: European Network for the Study of Adrenal Tumors — Adrenocortical Carcinoma Outcomes
landmarkTrials.population: 1,231 patients with ACC from 18 centers across Europe
landmarkTrials.finding: Complete (R0) resection was the strongest predictor of survival (5-year OS 70% vs 10% for R2). Mitotane improved OS in R0 patients with high Ki-67
landmarkTrials.impact: Defined optimal surgical extent (en bloc R0 resection) and the role of mitotane in ACC management
landmarkTrials.reference: Fassnacht M, et al. ENSAT. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(10):3925-3932

landmarkTrials.trial: FIRM-ACT: First-line Therapy for Advanced ACC
landmarkTrials.population: 304 patients with advanced ACC randomized to EDP + Mitotane vs Streptozocin + Mitotane
landmarkTrials.finding: EDP (Etoposide, Doxorubicin, Cisplatin) + Mitotane improved progression-free survival (5.0 vs 2.1 months), but no OS benefit (14.8 vs 12.0 months)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established EDP + Mitotane as first-line chemotherapy for advanced ACC
landmarkTrials.reference: Fassnacht M, et al. N Engl J Med. 2012;366(18):1739-1746

landmarkTrials.trial: PASO: Primary Aldosteronism Surgery Outcome Study
landmarkTrials.population: 705 patients from 12 centers who underwent adrenalectomy for Conn's syndrome
landmarkTrials.finding: Complete biochemical cure in 94% and complete clinical cure (normal BP off medications) in 37%. Partial clinical cure (reduced medications) in 47%
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that adrenalectomy is highly effective for Conn's, but long-standing hypertension may persist in some patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Williams TA, et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2017;5(9):689-699

landmarkTrials.trial: ADIUVO: Adjuvant Mitotane vs Observation in Low-Risk ACC
landmarkTrials.population: 91 patients with low-risk ACC (R0, Ki-67 <10%) randomized to mitotane vs observation
landmarkTrials.finding: Mitotane did not significantly improve recurrence-free survival (HR 0.61, 95% CI 0.29-1.28, p=0.19). High toxicity with mitotane (all patients had adverse events)
landmarkTrials.impact: Questioned routine adjuvant mitotane in low-risk ACC; individualized decision-making recommended
landmarkTrials.reference: Fassnacht M, et al. ESMO 2020 Virtual Congress, Abstract 300

landmarkTrials.trial: Shen WT, et al. Laparoscopic vs Open Adrenalectomy for Large Tumors
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 15 studies comparing laparoscopic vs open adrenalectomy for tumors >6cm
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic approach had lower blood loss, shorter hospital stay, fewer wound complications, but higher rates of capsular rupture (6% vs 2%) for large tumors
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is safe for tumors up to 10cm with benign features; open approach preferred for suspected ACC
landmarkTrials.reference: Shen WT, et al. World J Surg. 2012;36(10):2423-2430

evidenceTable.recommendation: All adrenal masses >1cm require hormonal workup to exclude pheochromocytoma, Conn's syndrome, and Cushing's syndrome
Pheochromocytoma requires pre-operative alpha-blockade (Phenoxybenzamine or Doxazosin) for 10-14 days before surgery
evidenceTable.reference: Manger WM, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 1999

evidenceTable.recommendation: Beta-blockade should only be added after adequate alpha-blockade to prevent unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction
Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is the gold standard for benign functional tumors and non-functional adenomas <6-10cm
evidenceTable.reference: Shen WT, World J Surg 2012; AAES Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Adrenocortical carcinoma requires open adrenalectomy with en bloc R0 resection and regional lymphadenectomy
evidenceTable.reference: ENSAT Registry, Fassnacht M, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2009

evidenceTable.recommendation: EDP (Etoposide, Doxorubicin, Cisplatin) + Mitotane is first-line chemotherapy for advanced ACC
evidenceTable.reference: FIRM-ACT Trial, Fassnacht M, N Engl J Med 2012

evidenceTable.recommendation: Adrenal vein sampling should guide lateralization in Conn's syndrome when CT shows bilateral or normal adrenals
evidenceTable.reference: PASO Study, Williams TA, Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2017

evidenceTable.recommendation: Non-functional adrenal adenomas <4cm with benign imaging features (HU <10) can be observed without surgery
Follow-up imaging at 6-12 months and annual hormonal workup for 4 years is recommended for observed non-functional adenomas
Genetic testing for RET, VHL, SDHB/D, NF1 should be offered to all patients with pheochromocytoma (especially if bilateral, multiple, or age <40)
evidenceTable.reference: AAES Guidelines; NGS for PPGL Consortium

evidenceTable.recommendation: Cortical-sparing adrenalectomy should be performed for bilateral pheochromocytoma (MEN2, VHL) to avoid steroid dependence
evidenceTable.reference: Castinetti F, et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2014

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Adrenal mass detected on imaging (CT/US/MRI) → Step 1: Determine if functional — Order: Plasma/24h urine fractionated metanephrines (pheo screen). Aldosterone/Renin ratio (Conn's screen). 1mg overnight DST or late-night salivary cortisol (Cushing's screen). DHEA-S, androstenedione (androgen screen). → Step 2: Characterize imaging — CT adrenal protocol: non-contrast HU <10 = benign adenoma; >10-20 = indeterminate; >20 = suspicious for malignancy. Absolute washout >60% = adenoma. Size: <4cm low risk, 4-6cm intermediate, >6cm high risk for ACC. → Step 3: Risk stratify — Functional tumor → surgery (prepare appropriately). Non-functional, <4cm, HU <10, benign features → observe. Non-functional, >4cm, HU >10, suspicious features → discuss adrenalectomy. Indeterminate → consider MRI chemical shift, or repeat CT at 6 months. → Step 4: Pre-operative preparation — Pheo: Phenoxybenzamine 10mg BD titrated up over 10-14 days. Add Propranolol 40mg TDS after alpha-block. High-sodium diet and IV fluids. Conn's: Spironolactone 25-100mg daily, correct K+. Cushing's: Stress-dose steroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h). → Step 5: Surgery — Laparoscopic adrenalectomy for benign tumors. Open adrenalectomy for ACC or large >10cm. → Step 6: Post-op — Monitor BP, glucose, electrolytes. Steroid taper for Cushing's. Confirm biochemical cure at 6 weeks. → Step 7: Follow-up — Non-functional observed: CT at 6-12 months, hormonal workup annually x4 years. Post-adrenalectomy: hormonal workup at 6 weeks to confirm cure. ACC: CT every 3-6 months.

managementPearls.context: The right adrenal vein is short and dangerous
managementPearls.pearl: The right adrenal vein is 4-6mm long and drains directly into the IVC from the posterior-lateral aspect. It is the 'Achilles heel' of right adrenalectomy. Avulsion causes torrential bleeding. Use a laparoscopic clip applier that fits comfortably; place clips with confidence and divide with scissors, not cautery near the IVC.
managementPearls.evidence: Gagner M, et al. Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Ann Surg 1997

managementPearls.context: The left adrenal vein is long and forgiving
managementPearls.pearl: The left adrenal vein drains into the left renal vein, often receiving the left inferior phrenic vein. It is 2-3cm long, giving you room to work. Ligate it early to reduce congestion. Remember: the tail of the pancreas and splenic vessels lie anterior to the left adrenal — retract them medially.
managementPearls.evidence: Gagner M, et al. Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Ann Surg 1997

managementPearls.pearl: This is the most important rule in pheochromocytoma management. Beta-blockade without alpha-blockade leaves alpha-1 receptors unopposed — vasoconstriction from norepinephrine can cause hypertensive crisis, pulmonary edema, or death. Always start alpha-blockade (Phenoxybenzamine or Doxazosin) first. Beta-blocker (Propranolol 40mg TDS) only for tachycardia after alpha-blockade established.
managementPearls.evidence: AAES Guidelines; Manger WM, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 1999

managementPearls.context: Conn's: AVS is essential with bilateral nodules
managementPearls.pearl: If CT shows bilateral nodules or normal-appearing adrenals in a patient with high ARR, adrenal vein sampling (AVS) is essential before surgery. Without lateralization, you risk removing the wrong gland. AVS is technically challenging (right adrenal vein cannulation has 70-80% success even in expert hands). Selectivity index >5 confirms successful cannulation.
managementPearls.evidence: PASO Study; Rossi GP, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2012

managementPearls.context: Cushing's: Steroid taper is the hardest part
managementPearls.pearl: Patients may feel that 'surgery was easy, but the steroid taper is brutal'. The contralateral adrenal gland has been suppressed for months/years. Plan a slow taper: Hydrocortisone 20mg mane + 10mg evening initially, reduce by 2.5-5mg every 2-4 weeks. The taper takes 6-18 months. Educate about 'sick day rules' — double or triple the dose during illness, injury, or surgery.
managementPearls.evidence: Nieman LK, et al. Cushing's syndrome guidelines. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2015

managementPearls.context: ACC: En bloc resection is critical
managementPearls.pearl: Adrenocortical carcinoma is aggressive and breaching the capsule during surgery significantly worsens prognosis. For any tumor >6cm or with suspicious features: plan an open approach, remove en bloc with periadrenal fat, Gerota's fascia, and regional lymph nodes. If the tumor invades kidney, spleen, pancreas, or diaphragm — resect the involved structures. R0 resection is the only chance for cure.
managementPearls.evidence: ENSAT; Fassnacht M, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2009

managementPearls.pearl: After the adrenal vein is ligated, catecholamine levels drop precipitously. Expect profound hypotension. Stop all alpha-blockers after vein ligation. Have vasopressors (norepinephrine, phenylephrine) drawn up and ready. These patients often need 12-24 hours of vasopressor support in ICU. Their blood vessels are 'floppy' after chronic alpha-blockade and the sudden loss of catecholamines.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Avulsion of the short right adrenal vein from the IVC during retraction, causing life-threatening hemorrhage
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a lateral approach with the liver retracted by a fan retractor held by an assistant (or a self-retaining retractor). Identify the IVC first. Follow the lateral border of the IVC superiorly. The adrenal vein is found at the postero-lateral aspect of the IVC, about 2cm above the renal vein. Dissect it carefully, preserving 5mm length on the gland side. Place TWO clips on the IVC side and ONE on the gland side.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the tail of the pancreas during medial retraction, causing pancreatitis or pancreatic leak
operativeNuances.expertTip: The tail of the pancreas is intimately associated with the anterior surface of the left adrenal. Key maneuver: divide the splenorenal ligament and reflect the spleen and tail of pancreas medially EN BLOC. Use a medial-to-lateral mobilization of the spleen. Once you see the psoas muscle posteriorly, you are in the correct plane behind the pancreas.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Excessive tumor manipulation causing catecholamine storm, hypertensive crisis, and arrhythmia
operativeNuances.expertTip: The golden rule: 'No touch technique' until the adrenal vein is ligated. Minimize grasping, squeezing, or retracting the adrenal gland directly. Dissect around the gland using the surrounding fat for retraction. Ligate the adrenal vein FIRST before extensive mobilization. Communicate constantly with the anesthesiologist about when you are about to ligate the vein.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Capsular rupture during mobilization of a large right ACC adherent to the liver and IVC
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a Chevron or Makuuchi incision (midline + right transverse extension) for adequate exposure. Approach the ACC from the LATERAL side, mobilizing the liver off the IVC first (Cherqui maneuver). Identify the right adrenal vein early and divide it. Then work posteriorly, separating the tumor from the diaphragm. Use the liver as a 'window' — do not retract the tumor directly.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Removing too much adrenal tissue, leaving the patient steroid-dependent, or removing too little and leaving behind tumor
operativeNuances.expertTip: The goal is to leave 20-30% of well-vascularized adrenal tissue. Use intraoperative ultrasound to locate the tumor and identify the extent. Dissect the tumor out with a 2-3mm margin of normal adrenal. Send the tumor for frozen section to confirm complete excision. Clip the arterial supply to the tumor but preserve the main adrenal vein if possible (it drains the remnant).

prognosis.mortality: Pheochromocytoma: operative mortality 0.5-1% with modern pre-op preparation (was 5-10% before alpha-blockade era). Conn's syndrome: <0.1% operative mortality, normal life expectancy after cure. Cushing's syndrome: 3-5x increased cardiovascular mortality if untreated; normalizes after successful treatment but residual metabolic effects may persist. ACC: 5-year overall survival 60-80% for Stage I-II, 30-40% for Stage III, <10% for Stage IV. Median survival for metastatic ACC: 12-15 months.
prognosis.morbidity: Pheochromocytoma: post-op hypotension requiring vasopressors (30-50%), transient hypoglycemia (10-20%), persistent hypertension in 25% (due to irreversible vascular remodeling). Conn's: persistent hypertension in 37% (especially if long-standing pre-op HTN). Cushing's: adrenal insufficiency requiring steroid replacement (100% initially, 30-50% may ultimately have recovery of HPA axis over 18-24 months). ACC: Cushing's syndrome if functional, tumor recurrence (50-80% within 5 years even after R0), chemotherapy toxicity.
prognosis.recurrence: Pheochromocytoma: 5-10% local recurrence, 5-10% metastatic (higher with SDHB mutation, large tumors, extra-adrenal). Conn's: <1% recurrence. Cushing's: <5% recurrence. ACC: 50-80% recurrence rate at 5 years after 'curative' resection. Median time to recurrence: 12-24 months. Most recurrences are within the first 2-3 years.
prognosis.survival: 5-year survival: Pheochromocytoma (benign) 95%, malignant pheochromocytoma 40-60%. Conn's: >95% (normal life expectancy). Cushing's: >95% after successful surgery, but residual cardiovascular risk persists for years. ACC: Stage I 80%, II 60%, III 35%, IV <10%. SDHB mutation carriers require lifelong surveillance for metastatic disease.
prognosis.reference: ENSAT Data; PASO Study; FIRM-ACT Trial; AAES Guidelines
